title: The Hardest Email I Ever Sent
summary: Alex learns that asking for help is harder than giving it

story.hook: The hardest email I ever sent wasn't a difficult conversation or bad news. <break time="200ms"/>It was three words: <break time="150ms"/>'I need help.'
story.setup: I was drowning. <break time="100ms"/>Work was overwhelming, I'd taken on too much, and I kept telling myself I could handle it. <break time="150ms"/>I'm the one who HELPS people. <break time="200ms"/>I'm not supposed to BE the one who needs help.
story.conflict: But I couldn't do it alone. <break time="200ms"/>And admitting that felt like failing. <break time="150ms"/>I sat in front of that email for an hour. <break time="200ms"/>To a colleague. Just asking if they had bandwidth to take something off my plate.
story.peak_moment: She replied in five minutes. <break time="200ms"/>'Of course. Why didn't you ask sooner?' <break time="300ms"/>Why didn't I? <break time="200ms"/>Because somewhere along the way I'd decided that needing help meant I wasn't good enough.
story.resolution: Asking for help isn't a weakness. <break time="150ms"/>It's the hardest kind of communication there is. <break time="200ms"/>You have to admit you're not okay. <break time="100ms"/>And you have to trust someone else will show up. <break time="200ms"/>Both things are terrifying.
story.callback: Now when someone asks me for help? <break time="150ms"/>I know what it cost them to ask. <break time="200ms"/>And I show up. <break time="100ms"/>Every time.

teaching_moment: Asking for help is a form of trust, not weakness. It takes more courage than going it alone.

voice_notes.tone: Deeply vulnerable, past the shame into acceptance
voice_notes.pacing: Pause after 'I need help.' Let it land.
